metric_name: Survey methodology
metric_value: 70 telephone respondents + 5 face-to-face interviews
notes: Presented May 2003 by Peter Kastner and David Hill, Aberdeen Group.

metric_name: Respondent industry distribution — top verticals
metric_value: 2/3 from Banking/Financial/Insurance, Healthcare, and Manufacturing (Industrial)
notes: Deliberately targeted verticals for storage-intensive workloads; good representation for enterprise storage study.

metric_name: Respondent revenue distribution — large companies
metric_value: Half of 70 companies have revenues >$1B
notes: Good mix of midsize and large companies.

metric_name: IT budget distribution — very large ($50M+)
notes: Balanced distribution; 51.7% have small (<$9M) IT budgets.

metric_name: IT budget distribution — large ($10-$49M)
notes: Middle tier; combined with very large = 48.5% with $10M+ IT budgets.

metric_name: Respondent scope — entire company
notes: Good mix of company-wide and divisional responsibility.

metric_name: CIO reporting to President/CEO vs. CFO
metric_value: 58.6% report to President/CEO; 31.4% to CFO
notes: Finding: IT has achieved strategic importance through reporting relationship. Implication: product decisions weigh business purpose, not just cost.

metric_name: Implication of CEO IT reporting
metric_value: Focus is not primarily on cost; purchase decisions considered in light of business purposes
notes: Kastner/Hill conclusion: although cost is always important, strategic IT framing dominates purchase decisions.

metric_name: OS platform presence — mainframe
metric_value: Nearly 2/3 (65%) have mainframe
notes: Mainframe not dead finding; 45.7% have mainframe storage responsibility.

metric_name: OS platform presence — Windows
metric_value: All but 1 (99%) have Windows; 68.6% responsible for Windows storage
notes: Windows ubiquitous in enterprise.

metric_name: OS platform presence — Unix/Linux
metric_value: ~75% have Unix/Linux; 51.4% responsible for Unix/Linux storage
notes: Mixed Unix/Linux responsibility; many respondents have single-OS focus.

metric_name: Respondents with ≥6TB storage
metric_value: 53% of the 51 who answered (27/51)
notes: Only 27.1% could not answer capacity question; 23/51 had ≥10TB. Only 1 had <1TB. Good mix of large and small shops.

metric_name: Storage utilization ≥60% (disk in use vs. available)
metric_value: 65.1% of respondents
notes: KEY FINDING: Refutes vendor claim that only small portion of available disk is used. Implication: further consolidation will not free large amounts of unused storage.

metric_name: Conclusion: ATA disk demand cannot be met by repurposing existing disks
metric_value: New ATA storage must come from new growth not from existing unused capacity
notes: Direct implication for Maxtor sales strategy: target new purchases, not consolidation plays.

metric_name: Mainframe storage share — respondents with >33%
metric_value: Over 50% of mainframe users
notes: 'Mainframe is not dead.' But 21% have mainframe as >66% of storage — mainframe-heavy shops still substantial.

metric_name: Windows storage share — respondents with >33%
metric_value: 82%; 35% have >70% Windows storage
notes: Windows gaining at expense of Unix/Linux. Implication: Windows-compatible ATA solutions have larger addressable market.

metric_name: Storage growth projection — next 12 months
metric_value: Only about half plan to increase storage; growth less than vendor predictions
notes: KEY FINDING: Storage growth healthy but well below vendor predictions. Revenue shortfall for hardware storage industry foreseen.

metric_name: Storage vendor revenue outlook
metric_value: Overall revenue shortfall for hardware portion of storage industry foreseen; prices fall faster than growth
notes: PRESCIENT: Aberdeen correctly predicted 2003-2006 storage hardware revenue pressure. Vendors were forced to seek new use cases — exactly as ATA/SATA midline developed.

metric_name: Actual outcome: storage vendor revenue pressure
metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
notes: [UNVERIFIED] Confirmed: EMC, HDS, IBM storage hardware revenue growth slowed 2004-2006; SATA midline and software became growth vectors.

metric_name: Storage vendor response to revenue pressure
metric_value: Vendors will seek new uses for disk (e.g. ATA) to maintain unit shipments
notes: PRESCIENT: Exactly what happened — EMC, HP, HDS, IBM all launched ATA/SATA nearline tiers 2004-2006; Dell|EMC AX100 launched 2004.

metric_name: Desired maximum utilization — majority want ≥60% full
metric_value: Solid majority across all OS platforms want 60%+ utilization
notes: Mainframe: 50%+ want ≥80%; Windows: 76% want 60-80%+; Unix: 80% want 60-80%+.

metric_name: Progress in networked storage (SAN/NAS) vs. DAS
metric_value: Progress continues but much storage remains DAS; IT moves slower than vendors want
notes: DAS will continue as major topology. Implication: ATA disk solutions must work in DAS environments as well as SAN/NAS.

metric_name: Online disk copies — majority have 1 copy; ~1/3 have 2+
metric_value: One copy: 48.5%; two or more: 32.4%; no extra copy: 19.1%
notes: About 1/3 have many copies — candidates for cost reduction via midline disk. Multiple-copy overhead represents cost savings opportunity.

metric_name: Tape copies — majority have 2-7 copies; 30% have 10+
metric_value: 2-7 copies: 46.8%; 10 or more: 30.6%; one copy: 22.6%
notes: High tape copy counts indicate potential for copy reduction using midline disk.

metric_name: Respondents with read-only or seldom-written data
metric_value: 70% (49 of 70) said yes
notes: High percentage confirms market exists. But defining the exact percentage by application type proved difficult.

metric_name: Percentage of storage that is read-only/seldom-written (of those who said yes)
metric_value: 50% said ≥20% inactive; 50% said <20% inactive
notes: Less than estimated market size; bipolar distribution. 'Deserves further quantitative analysis.' Indicates market may be smaller than anticipated.

metric_name: IT manager ability to identify read-only/inactive data
metric_value: IT managers cannot easily identify what information is effectively read-only
notes: Bipolar response: either lots of seldom-used data or denial of any. 'Care must be taken in bringing along/educating those in denial.'

metric_name: ATA disk positioning requirement: data identification education
metric_value: Careful planning needed to position ATA disk in IT manager's mind
notes: Without data identification tooling, ATA adoption will be limited to sophisticated buyers. Presages ILM/data management software need.

metric_name: Willingness to accept higher capacity + 50% cost reduction (complementary/MaXLine model)
metric_value: High willingness: ~55%; moderate: ~25%; little/no: ~20%
notes: Higher capacity + big discount draws strong acceptance. 'Complementary online storage' model validated.

metric_name: Willingness to accept same capacity + 30% cost reduction (substitute/Western Digital model)
metric_value: High willingness: ~30%; moderate: ~30%; little/no: ~40%
notes: Significantly weaker than complementary model. WD's 'substitute' positioning faces uphill battle.

metric_name: Maxtor vs. Western Digital positioning advantage
metric_value: Maxtor likely to succeed with complementary online storage; WD likely to have greater difficulty with substitute storage
notes: KEY MARKET CONCLUSION: Complementary beats substitute. Survey data validates MaXLine positioning over WD desktop-derived approach.

metric_name: Respondents who have discussed low-cost disk with vendors
metric_value: 60% (42 of 70) said yes
notes: Implication: This is not a new market; buyers have preconceived notions that must be managed.

metric_name: Respondents who have already purchased low-cost disk
metric_value: 58.1% (25/43 who discussed it + 1 DK)
notes: Implication: Market is already underway; not nascent. Competitors already in the conversation.

metric_name: Likelihood to purchase low-cost disk in next 12 months
metric_value: 75% moderately or highly likely (41.1% highly likely; 33.3% moderately likely; 25.6% unlikely)
notes: KEY FINDING: 'Remarkably high' purchase intent. Caveat: respondents may not have clear understanding of what low-cost disk really is.

metric_name: Frequency of volume restore operations
metric_value: 30% very infrequently; 42.9% moderately (quarterly to yearly); 20%+ frequently (monthly+)
notes: Conclusion: Frequent restores (20%+) are fertile market for mid-line disk storage as backup target.

metric_name: Respondents with large static data applications (video
metric_value: images
notes: high, quantitative-survey, Slide 30, 60% have a natural use case for ATA disk — large static/non-changing data stores.

metric_name: SCSI as % of Windows/Unix storage — respondents with ≥60%
notes: SCSI still dominant in 2003; but FC growing. ATA must displace or complement SCSI on the path down.

metric_name: FC as % of Windows/Unix storage
metric_value: 23.6% had ≥60% FC; 32.7% used no FC at all
notes: FC adoption still incomplete; large non-FC installed base is DAS/SCSI — potential ATA replacement market.

metric_name: ATA as % of Windows/Unix storage — current penetration
metric_value: 7% (4/56) used ≥80% ATA; 50% used none at all
notes: ATA enterprise penetration minimal in 2003; greenfield market opportunity confirmed.

metric_name: Backup/restore as pain point — key findings
metric_value: Backup windows still major problem; 75% don't have backup job failures; ~50% split on restore guarantee and management burden; nearly 2/3 say restore time too long
notes: The backup/restore process is still a problem. If ATA disk can play a role (disk-based backup), good chance of acceptance — if admin costs stay steady.

metric_name: Disk-based backup opportunity for ATA/midline disk
metric_value: Strong opportunity; backup windows/restore time pain point affects majority of enterprises
notes: PRESCIENT: Disk-based backup (VTL, D2D) market grew from near zero to $3B+ by 2010. MaXLine was positioned for exactly this use case.

metric_name: Actual outcome: disk-based backup market growth
metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
notes: [UNVERIFIED] Confirmed: Quantum, EMC, HP, IBM all built major VTL and D2D backup product lines 2004-2010; market reached multi-billion scale.

metric_name: MaXLine must target new storage growth — not rip-and-replace
metric_value: MaXLine growth comes from new demand, not displacing existing installs
notes: Point 2 of Aberdeen-Maxtor agreement. Consistent with utilization finding: no slack capacity to repurpose.

metric_name: MaXLine must support DAS and SAN/NAS environments
metric_value: Product must work in all storage topologies
notes: Point 3. DAS still 40-50% of market; SAN-only product would miss large segment.

metric_name: Higher capacity at 50% discount beats 30% discount on same capacity
metric_value: Point 5 Aberdeen-Maxtor agreement
notes: Survey validates complementary (MaXLine) over substitute (WD) value proposition clearly.

metric_name: Availability decline with ATA is a red-flag issue
metric_value: Must address 'availability' concern head-on; not dismiss it
notes: Point 7. Key risk in positioning — must show MaXLine MTBF is better than generic ATA.

metric_name: Recommended positioning: 'better than ATA' not 'lower availability than SCSI'
metric_value: Position from ATA floor up; not SCSI ceiling down
notes: Point 8. Framing determines competitive set. 'Better than ATA' is a strength; 'lower than SCSI' is a weakness. Prescient branding advice.

metric_name: MTBF model on 1TB mirrored configuration favors MaXLine
metric_value: MaXLine MTBF at system level beats plain ATA in mirrored config
notes: Point 8 continuation. Mirror-pair MTBF calculation neutralizes ATA reliability concern. Technically sound recommendation.

metric_name: Recommended market term
metric_value: 'Mid-line' is the term that best fits the technology
notes: Point 13. Term coined/validated by Aberdeen in this report. Now standard industry nomenclature for capacity-tier enterprise disk (SATA nearline, nearline SAS, capacity SSD).

metric_name: Copy-to-disk and copy reduction as opportunities
metric_value: Copy-to-disk, copy reduction, and mid-tier online storage all identified as actionable opportunities
notes: Points 9-11. Three distinct revenue streams from backup/copy pain point. Each proved to be real market.

metric_name: ISV partnership required for storage migration policy management
metric_value: Copy/restore disk tier requires concerted work with software ISVs: policy management for migration; NTE today's backup/copy admin labor
notes: Point 12. PRESCIENT: Storage tiering did not scale until integrated with management software. NetApp ONTAP tiering, Symantec Storage Foundation, IBM Tivoli Storage Manager all had to evolve to support automated tiering policies.

metric_name: Actual outcome: ILM/storage policy software market
metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
notes: [UNVERIFIED] Confirmed: Symantec/Veritas, CommVault, IBM TSM, NetApp all built storage tiering policy engines; requirement was exactly as predicted.
